title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of Mexican cuisine with our authentic Chicken Mole recipe! Learn to make rich, flavorful Pollo en Mole sauce from scratch. Perfect for dinner!
longTailKeywords: best chicken mole sauce from scratch
seo.h1: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
h2Headings: Step-by-Step: How to Make Chicken Mole
Tips for the Best Pollo en Mole
seo.imageAlt: A plate of authentic Mexican Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) garnished with sesame seeds and served with rice.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the rich, complex flavors of traditional Mexican Chicken Mole, a culinary delight.

description: Pollo en Mole is a culinary masterpiece, a rich and complex Mexican chicken dish bathed in a velvety, dark sauce. This traditional recipe combines an array of chiles, spices, nuts, and a hint of chocolate to create a flavor profile that is both sweet and savory. It is a true celebration of Mexican heritage, perfect for special occasions or a comforting family meal.

ingredients.notes: or a small piece of day-old bread

instructions.title: Cook the Chicken
instructions.text: Place the chicken pieces in a large pot and cover with chicken broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 25-30 minutes, or until cooked through. Reserve 2 cups of the cooking liquid. Once cooled, remove skin and bones if desired, then shred or keep chicken pieces whole. Set aside.

instructions.title: Prepare the Chiles
instructions.text: Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the dried chiles for 30 seconds per side until fragrant, being careful not to burn them. Place the toasted chiles in a bowl and cover with hot water. Let them rehydrate for 20 minutes until soft. Drain and set aside.

instructions.title: Sauté Aromatics and Fry Ingredients
instructions.text: In a large skillet, heat 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil over medium heat. Add the tomatoes, onion, and garlic. Cook until softened and slightly charred, about 5-7 minutes. Remove and set aside. In the same skillet, add another tablespoon of oil. Fry the tortillas and bread until golden brown and crispy. Remove and set aside. Then, lightly toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, raisins, cumin, coriander, cinnamon stick, cloves, and peppercorns for 2-3 minutes until fragrant. Remove and set aside.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Combine the rehydrated chiles, sautéed aromatics, fried tortillas and bread, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, and the reserved 2 cups of chicken cooking liquid in a blender. Blend until very smooth, adding more chicken broth if needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl, pressing on the solids to extract all liquid. Discard the remaining solids.

instructions.title: Simmer the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat the remaining 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil over medium heat. Pour in the strained mole sauce. Bring to a simmer, stirring constantly to prevent sticking. Reduce heat to low and continue to simmer for at least 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens and deepens in color. The oil should separate slightly, indicating the sauce is well cooked.

instructions.title: Finish the Mole
instructions.text: Stir in the Mexican chocolate and sugar. Continue to cook, stirring until the chocolate is fully melted and incorporated. Season with salt to taste. The mole should have a balanced sweet and savory flavor. Add the cooked chicken pieces to the mole sauce and gently stir to coat. Simmer for another 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors to meld and the chicken to heat through.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Serve the Pollo en Mole hot, garnished with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. It pairs wonderfully with Mexican rice, warm corn tortillas, or a simple side of refried beans. Enjoy this authentic taste of Mexico!

tips: For a truly authentic flavor, toast your chiles and spices individually until fragrant, being careful not to burn them, as this develops their deep, complex notes.
If you cant find Mexican chocolate, use unsweetened dark chocolate (70% cocoa or higher) and add a pinch of sugar to balance the bitterness.
Mole sauce improves with age; make it a day ahead and gently reheat it before serving to allow the flavors to meld even further.
article:
Pollo en Mole: A Journey into Mexico's Culinary Heart

Mexican cuisine is a vibrant tapestry of flavors, textures, and aromas, celebrated worldwide for its depth and diversity. Among its most iconic and revered dishes is Pollo en Mole, or Chicken Mole. More than just a meal, mole is a culinary masterpiece, a symbol of Mexican heritage, and a dish often reserved for special occasions and family gatherings. Its complexity, born from a symphony of ingredients, offers a unique taste experience that lingers long after the last bite. If you're ready to embark on a rewarding culinary adventure, mastering this authentic Chicken Mole Recipe is a journey worth taking.

## What is Chicken Mole?

At its heart, "mole" (pronounced MOH-lay) is not a single dish but a category of rich, intricate sauces in Mexican cuisine. While many variations exist, the most famous is Mole Poblano, often served with chicken. The word "mole" derives from the Nahuatl word "mulli," meaning "sauce." Unlike simple gravies, mole sauces are renowned for their incredible depth, created by blending numerous ingredients – typically dried chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, fruits, and a touch of chocolate.

The history of mole is as rich as its flavor. Its origins are steeped in legend, with stories dating back to pre-Hispanic times, and further evolving with the introduction of European ingredients during the colonial era. Monasteries in Puebla and Oaxaca are often credited with refining the mole as we know it today. The resulting sauce is a harmonious balance of smoky, sweet, spicy, and earthy notes, making it truly unforgettable. It’s a dish that embodies the soul of Mexican cooking, a testament to patience and the art of flavor layering.

## Ingredients for Authentic Chicken Mole

Creating an authentic Chicken Mole Recipe requires a commitment to quality ingredients. While the list might seem long, each component plays a crucial role in building the sauce's characteristic complexity.

**For the Chicken:**
*   **Chicken pieces:** 8-10 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks (or a whole cut-up chicken). Bone-in chicken adds more flavor to the broth.
*   **Onion:** 1 large, quartered
*   **Garlic:** 4-5 cloves, smashed
*   **Salt:** To taste
*   **Bay leaves:** 2

**For the Mole Sauce (the "Pastel de Mole"):**
*   **Dried Chiles:**
    *   4-5 Ancho chiles (fruity, mild heat)
    *   3-4 Mulato chiles (smoky, slightly sweet)
    *   3-4 Pasilla chiles (earthy, dark fruit notes)
    *   1-2 Chipotle Morita chiles (smoky, moderate heat – optional for extra kick)
*   **Aromatics & Vegetables:**
    *   1 medium white onion, roughly chopped
    *   4-5 cloves garlic, peeled
    *   2 ripe tomatoes, roughly chopped
    *   1-2 tomatillos, husked and chopped (for acidity)
    *   1 plantain, ripe (yellow with black spots), sliced
*   **Nuts & Seeds (toasted):**
    *   1/4 cup almonds
    *   1/4 cup peanuts
    *   2 tablespoons sesame seeds (plus more for garnish)
    *   1 tablespoon pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
*   **Spices:**
    *   1 tablespoon whole black peppercorns
    *   1/2 teaspoon whole cloves
    *   1/2 teaspoon ground cinnamon (or a 1-inch stick)
    *   1/2 teaspoon ground cumin
    *   1/4 teaspoon star anise (optional, for licorice notes)
    *   1/2 teaspoon dried oregano (Mexican oregano preferred)
*   **Thickeners/Sweeteners:**
    *   2-3 stale corn tortillas, torn into pieces, or 1 slice of stale bread
    *   1/4 cup raisins
    *   1-2 tablespoons piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar
    *   1-2 ounces Mexican chocolate (bittersweet, often with cinnamon), chopped
*   **Liquid:**
    *   4-6 cups reserved chicken broth (from cooking chicken)
*   **Fat:**
    *   2-3 tablespoons lard or vegetable oil
*   **Salt:** To taste

## Step-by-Step: How to Make Chicken Mole

Making Pollo en Mole is a multi-step process, but each stage builds upon the last, culminating in an incredibly flavorful sauce. Here’s how to prepare this classic Chicken Mole Recipe:

**1. Prepare the Chicken:**
   *   Place chicken pieces in a large pot with quartered onion, smashed garlic, bay leaves, and a generous pinch of salt. Cover with water.
   *   Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until the chicken is tender and cooked through.
   *   Remove chicken, set aside. Strain the broth and reserve 4-6 cups for the mole sauce.

**2. Prepare the Chiles:**
   *   Wipe dried chiles clean. Remove stems and shake out most of the seeds (a few seeds are okay for flavor).
   *   Heat a dry skillet over medium heat. Toast chiles in batches for 30-60 seconds per side until fragrant and slightly pliable (do not burn, or they will become bitter).
   *   Place toasted chiles in a bowl, cover with hot water (or some of the reserved chicken broth), and let rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft.

**3. Toast & Fry Aromatics, Nuts, and Spices:**
   *   In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat 1 tablespoon of lard or oil over medium heat.
   *   Add almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds. Toast until golden and fragrant, stirring constantly. Remove and set aside.
   *   In the same pot, add more lard/oil if needed. Fry the chopped onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomatillos, and plantain until softened and slightly caramelized. Remove and set aside.
   *   Lightly toast the whole peppercorns, cloves, cinnamon stick (if using), and star anise until fragrant (about 1 minute). Add ground cumin and oregano briefly.

**4. Blend the Sauce:**
   *   Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving a little of the soaking liquid.
   *   Combine the rehydrated chiles, fried aromatics (onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomatillos, plantain), toasted nuts and seeds, toasted spices, raisins, piloncillo/brown sugar, and torn tortillas/bread in a blender.
   *   Add 2-3 cups of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until very smooth. This may require blending in batches. If the sauce is too thick, add a little more chicken broth or chile soaking liquid. For an extra smooth sauce, pass it through a fine-mesh sieve (though this is optional for a more rustic texture).

**5. Simmer the Sauce:**
   *   In the same pot you used earlier (cleaned), heat 2 tablespoons of lard or oil over medium heat.
   *   Carefully pour the blended mole sauce into the hot fat. Be cautious, as it will splatter.
   *   Bring the sauce to a simmer, stirring constantly for the first 5-10 minutes to prevent sticking. This frying step is crucial for deepening the flavor and color of the mole.
   *   Reduce heat to low, add the chopped Mexican chocolate, and stir until melted and incorporated.
   *   Continue to simmer the mole, stirring occasionally, for at least 30-60 minutes (or up to 2-3 hours for richer flavor), adding more chicken broth as needed to reach your desired consistency. The sauce should thicken and darken, and the flavors will meld beautifully. Season with salt to taste.

**6. Combine Chicken and Sauce:**
   *   Gently add the cooked chicken pieces to the simmering mole sauce.
   *   Simmer for another 15-20 minutes, allowing the chicken to absorb the rich flavors of the mole.

## Tips for the Best Pollo en Mole

*   **Quality Chiles:** Use fresh, pliable dried chiles. Avoid brittle or dusty ones.
*   **Don't Burn:** When toasting chiles, nuts, and spices, watch them carefully. Burnt ingredients will impart bitterness.
*   **Blend Until Smooth:** A powerful blender is key. For the smoothest sauce, blend in smaller batches and consider straining.
*   **Simmer Time is Essential:** Mole benefits greatly from a long, slow simmer. This allows the flavors to fully develop and integrate. Don't rush this step.
*   **Balance the Flavors:** Taste and adjust. If it's too spicy, a bit more chocolate or piloncillo can help. If it lacks depth, a pinch more salt or a longer simmer can work wonders.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole often tastes even better the next day, as the flavors continue to meld. It's a fantastic make-ahead dish.
*   **Heat Control:** If the sauce is too thick, thin it with more chicken broth. If it's too thin, let it simmer uncovered until it reduces.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Pollo en Mole is traditionally served with warm corn tortillas and a side of fluffy Mexican rice (Arroz Rojo). The rice helps to soak up every drop of that incredible sauce. For garnish, a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds and finely chopped fresh cilantro or onion adds a lovely touch.

Beyond the classic, you can also serve your Chicken Mole with:
*   Refried beans
*   Roasted vegetables like zucchini or sweet potatoes
*   Tamales, using the mole as a filling or topping
*   As an enchilada sauce

For drinks, a cold Mexican lager, a dry red wine, or a refreshing agua fresca (like horchata or jamaica) would complement the complex flavors beautifully.

## Common Questions & Variations

**Can I make mole vegetarian?**
Absolutely! Simply omit the chicken, use vegetable broth, and consider adding roasted mushrooms, sweet potatoes, or plantains to the sauce for heartiness.

**Can I freeze mole sauce?**
Yes, mole sauce freezes exceptionally well. Make a large batch and freeze portions for quick and easy meals later. It can last for several months in the freezer.

**What kind of chocolate should I use?**
Mexican chocolate, often found in disc or tablet form and typically flavored with cinnamon, is ideal. If you can't find it, a good quality bittersweet chocolate (70% cacao or higher) will work, adding a pinch of cinnamon if desired.

**Are there different types of mole?**
Yes, Mexico boasts a vast array of moles! While Mole Poblano (the one described here, often reddish-brown) is famous, Oaxaca alone is known as "the land of seven moles," including:
*   **Mole Negro:** The darkest and often most complex, featuring many chiles and a deep, rich flavor.
*   **Mole Colorado/Rojo:** Similar to Poblano, with a vibrant red hue.
*   **Mole Amarillo:** A yellow mole, lighter in color and often thickened with masa.
*   **Mole Verde:** A fresh, vibrant green mole made with fresh herbs, tomatillos, and green chiles.

**Can I use a shortcut?**
While this Chicken Mole Recipe is a labor of love, for those short on time, good quality store-bought mole paste is available. Just dilute it with chicken broth, simmer, and add your cooked chicken. However, nothing quite compares to the depth and freshness of homemade.

Embracing the process of making Pollo en Mole is a culinary journey that connects you to centuries of Mexican tradition. The rich aroma filling your kitchen and the incredible flavor of the finished dish are truly rewarding. So, gather your ingredients, set aside some time, and prepare to create a masterpiece that will delight your senses and impress your loved ones.
